pageTitle.value: Bernardine Evaristo & Ayesha Harruna Attah - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: After finishing her studies in the US, author Ayesha Harruna Attah returned to Africa with a mission to write African stories for African readers. Five books later, she is ambitious for a wider readership.

WaPageTitle.value: Bernardine Evaristo & Ayesha Harruna Attah - Rolex mentoring programme

metadescription.value: Mentor Bernardine Evaristo is helping author Ayesha Harruna Attah bridge the gap and bring African stories to a global audience. More on rolex.org.

previewTitle.value: Bernardine Evaristo & Ayesha Harruna Attah - Rolex mentoring programme

text.value: After finishing her studies in the US, author Ayesha Harruna Attah returned to Africa with a mission to write African stories for African readers. Five books later, she is ambitious for a wider readership. Booker Prize-winning author Bernardine Evaristo is advising her about how to bridge the gap between her remote life in Senegal and the opportunities available in international publishing hubs. Here, they each relate how they experienced their relationship.

“I’ve written since I can remember. I write from a primal urge – it’s the way I breathe now. When I’m not writing, I feel off-kilter.

I am from Accra, the capital of Ghana, where my parents had a newspaper, and before that they had a literary magazine, so I grew up surrounded by creatives. Reading was also a big part of my upbringing – the highlight of my week was going to the library with my mom.

I don’t care about putting the African continent on the map as much as I care about Africans knowing themselves and their stories. But what I find most frustrating is that across Africa itself, the publishing opportunities are so small.

I would like for my books to go to the African reader but then to travel widely. But after five books, I am not getting the reach that I want. I want to reach the whole world. I was asking myself if I was in the wrong field. I was on the precipice, so ready to give up. To know that Bernardine had been through similar experiences is encouraging. What maybe differs is that she was always confident, and just stayed confident. That’s a lesson for me. To have that belief in yourself, even as other people are not seeing it, and to keep going – that is inspiring. One of the things she’s helping me do is to dream big and to dare.

With Bernardine, it’s a combination of editorial conversations, and career building – introducing me to lots of people. She has opened up her world to me. We went to the theatre in London, as she wanted to introduce this art form to me, and then she invited me to a literary festival in Trinidad to meet other black artists. She’s very connected.

She has a really great sense of humour. And yet she’s talking about serious things. She is a writer who can play with language, bend genres, make you laugh, make you think, make you stop. That’s who I want to be.”

title.value: She has a really great sense of humour. And yet she’s talking about serious things. She is a writer who can play with language, bend genres, make you laugh, make you think, make you stop. That’s who I want to be.

text.value:
“When I was 12 years old, I became involved in acting, which led me to train to be an actor, but also to write for theatre. I lost my passion for acting eventually but continued writing and in time started publishing books. Ten books on, here I am today. I am driven, obviously, by my innate creativity, but also by the desire to explore the African diaspora from a British perspective.

A lot of African writers leave the continent and live in America, the UK, Germany, or wherever. Sometimes that shapes what they write, whereas Ayesha lives in Senegal, and writes about the history of the continent, which is still under-explored in literature.

We are very different writers; she explores history from as authentic a perspective as possible, whereas I’m a very experimental writer. Even though I write about deep history, I also mix in the contemporary by playing around with time and space. I love being anachronistic.

Ayesha, who’s written five novels, is a serious craftswoman. I’ve been able to support her and encourage her to believe more in her considerable talent and to offer her in-depth feedback on her novel in progress. We’ve talked about how to bring history alive in such a way that it is relatable to people today. And how to write characters who share our emotional world because whether they lived 3,000 years ago, or are in the modern age, we’re still essentially the same fully evolved human beings.

title.value: We are very different writers; she explores history from as authentic a perspective as possible, whereas I’m a very experimental writer.

text.value:
Most people would admit that they want to have successful careers as writers and that means reaching readers and audiences, the larger the better. It’s very hard to achieve this if you are a literary writer. It took me many decades to become a bestseller.

We have practical discussions about how to survive as a writer and the importance of networking and developing friendships across the world, especially as Ayesha is working from a tiny village in Senegal and not a big metropolis.

How do you build those networks remotely? It’s important to go to festivals and literary events, and to support other writers. We belong to the literature ecosystem – we receive but we must also give. For many writers, networking is essential to building a career. You cannot do it alone.

You might look at literary stars and think somehow they had it easy, perhaps because they come from a certain background, or have a natural talent, and the writing just appeared fully formed for them. We shouldn’t make these assumptions. Many of the most successful writers have had to struggle – with their creativity, confidence, time-management, other responsibilities and inevitable career highs and lows.”

pageTitle.value: Anne Lacaton & Arine Aprahamian - Rolex mentoring programme

pageCaption.value: Young Lebanese-Armenian architect Arine Aprahamian is inspired to create buildings that are innovative, affordable and sustainable. Her philosophy sits well with French Pritzker-Prize winning architect Anne Lacaton, who espouses building renewal over demolition.
